revision_history: v1 (2026-05-15, Stage 1): initial draft from radar-accumulated coverage + 33 cited sources.

v2 (2026-05-15, Stage 6): integrated Stages 2-5. Major corrections to reframe + view cards; new Economic fact card on petroleum exclusion; AGC scope expanded; Article IX citation dropped; 'joint legal review' phrasing removed; structural asymmetry surfaced without manufacturing federal voices.

headline: Sabah paused a federal rollout. Federal courts paused Sabah's 40% payout.
context: On 13 May 2026, Sabah deferred the federal border agency (AKPS) rollout citing MA63 immigration autonomy. On 6 April, the Court of Appeal granted Putrajaya a stay on Sabah's 40% revenue — a case the High Court ruled had gone 48 years without the mandatory review.

cards.big: Sabah just paused a federal rollout. Federal courts paused Sabah's 40% payout 5 weeks earlier.
cards.sub: On 13 May Sabah deferred AKPS. On 6 April the Court of Appeal stayed payment on a 40% grant the High Court ruled had gone 48 years without review.
revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 3 — MISLEADING): removed 'Both sides cite MA63' from sub. Stage 3 verified that the Court of Appeal stay reasoning did not itself invoke MA63 (only the underlying revenue case is about Article 112C/112D). The sub now anchors on the verifiable High Court finding (48-year non-review) instead.
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5 — view-card cowardice extends to hook): added '48 years without review' to the sub so the structural backdrop is visible from the first card, not hidden in the view card.

cards.big: On 6 April 2026 the Court of Appeal granted Putrajaya's stay on Sabah's 40% revenue payment pending appeal.
cards.sub: AGC said the appeal contests the High Court's finding of breach by BOTH governments since 1974 — not the 40% formula itself.
revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 3 — omitted_fact): expanded AGC scope. Stage 3 noted the AGC clarification did not only say the formula was 'untouched' — it also said the appeal concerns constitutional interpretation and findings that BOTH Federal AND Sabah Governments breached constitutional obligations since 1974. The new sub captures this mutual-breach framing.

REMOVED (Stage 3 — UNVERIFIED_DETAIL): removed direct 'fait accompli' quote from the sub. Stage 3 set m_true 0.68 for the verbatim quote because the Court of Appeal grounds were not located. The substantive reasoning is preserved without putting an unverified verbatim in the card.

REPHRASED (Stage 2 — UNVERIFIED 'untouched'): removed verbatim 'untouched' from this card. Stage 2 flagged it as a Malay Mail headline paraphrase, not a verified AGC verbatim. The substantive AGC position (appeal is not against the formula) is retained in the sub.

cards.big: On 13 May 2026 Sabah deferred the AKPS rollout, citing clashes between Act 860 (2024) and the Immigration Act 1959/63.
cards.sub: CM Hajiji called Sabah's MA63 immigration autonomy 'non-negotiable'; under Section 6(3) of the AKPS Act the state would have only indirect oversight of the federal agency.
revisions: VERIFIED (Stage 3): card retained substantively unchanged. Stage 3 set m_true 0.85 for the deferral fact and 0.9 for Hajiji's 'non-negotiable' wording, sourced to the official Sabah CM press statement (https://cm.sabah.gov.my/siaran-media/7017).

DROPPED from sources (Stage 3 — INCORRECT): removed 'Article IX provisions' citation from the source list. Stage 3 verified that Article IX of the Agreement Relating to Malaysia concerns common market and financial arrangements with Singapore, not immigration. Immigration provisions sit elsewhere in the agreement and in the Immigration Act 1959/63 itself. The card body never cited 'Article IX' — only the Stage 1 source notes did, so the card body is unaffected, but the synthesis source list is corrected.

cards.big: The 40% formula is constitutional, but it excludes Sabah's largest revenue stream: oil and gas, ring-fenced under the Petroleum Development Act 1974.
cards.sub: Articles 112C/112D cover 'net revenue derived from Sabah.' Oil and gas sit under Petronas and a 5% royalty arrangement, not the 40% special grant.
revisions: ADDED (Stage 4 — importance 0.9 missing fact): Stage 4 flagged the petroleum exclusion as the strongest omission. Without it, readers cannot understand why the 40% case matters so much: even a full win for Sabah on Article 112D would not capture the largest single resource flow.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): the petroleum-exclusion claim was verified against Aliran, Borneo Post, and the Petroleum Development Act 1974 statutory text (https://bhess.jpm.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/Petroleum-Development-Act-1974-Act-144.pdf). The 5% royalty arrangement to producing states is independently documented.

cards.big: Within 48 hours the Sabah Law Society, the state immigration officers' union, and ex-CM Salleh Said Keruak backed the AKPS deferral.
cards.sub: SLS called it 'legal foresight'. Salleh called it 'prudent and justified'. Both said MA63 immigration autonomy is constitutional, not administrative.
revisions: VERIFIED (Stage 3): card retained substantively unchanged. Stage 3 set m_true 0.82-0.85 across the backing claims, sourced to Borneo Post (SLS, KPPIS) and Jesselton Times (Salleh).

COMPRESSED (Stage 6): tightened 'a constitutional condition of Malaysia's formation' to 'constitutional, not administrative' to fit the bite-size budget without dropping the load-bearing distinction — namely that Sabah-side backers frame autonomy as enforceable legal right, not a discretionary administrative arrangement.

cards.big: The High Court found both governments in breach since 1974. Putrajaya pauses the court's remedy; Sabah pauses a new federal law before it operates.
revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 2 + Stage 3 — both flagged the same issue as the #1 cardinal sin): the original reframe attributed 'erosion' to 'federal voices'. This was demonstrably wrong — the 'erosion' framing came from Sabah-side actors (Warisan reps, KPPIS, Sabah Government), not federal spokespersons. Stage 2 called it 'sophisticated misleading framing'; Stage 3 marked it INCORRECT with m_false 0.75.

STRENGTHENED (Stage 5 — view-and-reframe cowardice): the new reframe acknowledges what Stage 5 said the cards were dodging — that one side is pausing a court remedy for its own 48-year breach, while the other is pausing a new federal law it disputes. This is structural asymmetry, not labelling asymmetry.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): the wording 'both governments in breach since 1974' is independently verified — it tracks the AGC's own characterisation (per Stage 3 omitted_fact) that the appeal contests the High Court's findings of breach by 'both federal and Sabah governments' since 1974. The reframe attributes the finding to the court, not to either political side.

cards.big: Two pauses, two clocks. Putrajaya's pause extends a 48-year delay the court called unconstitutional. Sabah's pause stops a new law before it operates.
revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 3 — MISLEADING): removed 'both moves are within process'. Stage 3 set m_true only 0.5 for that claim because Sabah's deferment has not been independently adjudicated, so calling it 'within process' overclaims.
REMOVED (Stage 3 — UNVERIFIED): removed 'joint legal review' phrasing. Stage 3 confirmed this phrase appears only in Jesselton Times framing, not in the official Sabah CM statement.

STRENGTHENED (Stage 5 — weakest_card flag on card 6): the new view explicitly acknowledges what Stage 5 said was being flattened — that the two pauses are not equivalent on the clock. One extends a 48-year breach; the other prevents a new statute from running. The structural imbalance is now visible.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): the phrase 'the court called unconstitutional' is verified against the High Court ruling — Judge Celestina Stuel Galid's 17 Oct 2025 judgment held that Putrajaya's failure to conduct the Article 112D review for 1974-2021 was a breach of constitutional duty (FMT, The Star).

stage_scores_applied.pa_rationale: Raised from 78 to 84 after applying Stage 3 corrections (AGC scope expanded; Article IX dropped; 'joint legal review' removed) and Stage 4 addition (petroleum-exclusion fact card). Capped at 84 (not higher) because Court of Appeal grounds and AGC press release still not pulled from primary documents — the legal-mechanism story is now significantly stronger but two source gaps remain.
stage_scores_applied.ba_rationale: Stage 2 raw bias_score 35 inverts to ba 65. After fixing the reframe misattribution (the single highest-severity flag), ba rises to 80. Residual 20 reflects (a) source-spectrum imbalance toward Sabah-press, which Stage 2 still flags as latent bias, and (b) the analysis remains state-rights-leaning even after correction.
stage_scores_applied.fc_rationale: Stage 3 raw factual_accuracy 74. Post-correction: 4 of 6 cardinal-sin flags from Stage 3 are now resolved (MISLEADING_FRAMING on 'both delays invoke same 1963 agreement' — removed; INCORRECT on 'federal voices erosion' — rewritten; OVERCLAIM on 'within process' — rewritten; UNVERIFIED on 'joint legal review' — removed). Two residual flags persist as flagged risks: Section 6(3) statutory text not directly pulled, and Court of Appeal grounds not pulled. fc rises to 84.
stage_scores_applied.af_rationale: Stage 4 completeness 65. Post-addition of the petroleum-exclusion fact card (Stage 4's importance-0.9 omission), af rises to 75. Sarawak/indigenous/women/B40 perspectives remain absent in the published cards — that is a deliberate scope choice (the issue is about the two pauses, not the full devolution debate), but it is also why af does not rise higher.
stage_scores_applied.ct_rationale: Stage 5 raw courage_score 31. Post-rewriting reframe + view to acknowledge structural asymmetry (48-year non-review on one side; pre-existing reservation on the other), ct rises to 60. Stage 5's harder demands — to name elite mutual self-interest and ordinary-Sabahan costs — remain unaddressed in the cards because Stage 5 itself tagged those as MY_OPINION, not verified primary sources. T4A discipline requires verified-only.
stage_scores_applied.sr_rationale: Synthesis applied 4 of 6 of Stage 3's cardinal-sin flags, Stage 2's single highest-severity flag, Stage 4's importance-0.9 omission, and Stage 5's strongest critique on the view card. Two over-integration decisions made explicitly (see synthesis_notes). No claim in the final cards is INTRODUCED-without-verification.

introduced_claims.claim: The 40% formula excludes oil and gas, ring-fenced under the Petroleum Development Act 1974; producing states receive a 5% royalty arrangement.
introduced_claims.verified_against: Petroleum Development Act 1974 (Act 144) statutory text; Aliran 'Sabah's 40% entitlement: Why Putrajaya's delay tactics won't work'; Borneo Post 'Sabah's 40% revenue formula does not include oil and gas revenue says state minister' (2020-04-15). Stage 6 web search 2026-05-15 confirmed the structural design: PDA 1974 captures petroleum revenue at federal level via Petronas; producing states receive a 5% cash payment.

introduced_claims.claim: Reframe wording: 'The High Court found both governments in breach since 1974.'
introduced_claims.verified_against: Stage 3 omitted_fact: AGC clarification 6 April 2026 said the federal appeal contests the High Court's findings that BOTH federal and Sabah governments breached constitutional obligations since 1974 (Malay Mail; The Edge Malaysia node/798778). The phrasing tracks the court's own finding as accepted by the AGC — neither side is editorially favoured.

introduced_claims.claim: View wording: '48-year delay the court called unconstitutional'.
introduced_claims.verified_against: Judge Datuk Celestina Stuel Galid 17 Oct 2025 judgment found the Federal Government's failure to conduct the Article 112D review for 1974-2021 was unlawful and in breach of constitutional duty. FMT 17 Oct 2025; The Star 18 Oct 2025; eLaw case report JE_2025_46.

introduced_claims.claim: Headline-sub wording: 'a 40% grant the High Court ruled had gone 48 years without review'.
introduced_claims.verified_against: Same 17 Oct 2025 judgment + eLaw case report. The High Court characterised 1974-2021 as the 'Lost Years' in which no Article 112D review took place.

synthesis_notes:
Two over-integration decisions made explicitly, not averaged:

(1) Stage 2 flagged the reframe as a MISLEADING_FRAMING because it manufactured 'federal voices'; Stage 5 flagged the view as cowardly because it flattened the asymmetry. Both critiques converge on the same diagnosis from opposite directions: the original cards both manufactured symmetry where the evidence shows asymmetry. The synthesis fix is single and clean — surface the structural asymmetry the court already found (mutual breach since 1974), without manufacturing federal spokespersons. This addresses both stages' core concerns simultaneously without compromising T4A's process-not-people discipline.

(2) Stage 5 demanded the cards name 'elite mutual self-interest' across Putrajaya and Kota Kinabalu, and the citizen-level costs to ordinary Sabahans. Stage 5 itself tagged both of these as MY_OPINION (not VERIFIED), with empty primary_source fields. T4A's Accuracy Standard requires every published claim to trace to a primary source. The synthesis therefore declined to introduce those framings into the cards, but acknowledges Stage 5's structural point through the verified-sourceable 'two clocks' framing in the view card. This is the right place to draw the line: structural asymmetry is sourceable to the High Court judgment; elite-self-interest framing is not.

Compression decisions: Card 1 sub dropped the verbatim 'fait accompli' quote because Stage 3 set m_true only 0.68 (Court of Appeal grounds not pulled). The substantive reasoning (federal government accepts entitlement, disputes calculation, doesn't want pre-appeal payment) is preserved in the AGC clarification framing. Card 5 (Political) compressed Salleh + SLS quotes by joining 'constitutional condition of Malaysia's formation' to the tighter 'constitutional, not administrative' — preserves the legal-vs-discretionary distinction.

Things deliberately NOT added: Sarawak perspective (Stage 4), indigenous-community perspective (Stage 4), gender impact (Stage 4 + Stage 5), B40 delivery question (Stage 4 + Stage 5), elite mutual-interest framing (Stage 5), AKPS exclusion of Sarawak detail. These are scope-out-of-scope for an issue about two specific pauses in April-May 2026. They belong in a separate, future issue on devolution outcomes, not this one. The af and ct scores reflect the cost of that scope choice.

final_self_check.four_cardinal_sins: none after corrections; see introduced_claims for Stage 6 verifications
final_self_check.overclaim_check: View no longer claims 'both moves are within process'. Reframe no longer manufactures 'federal voices'. The court's own finding (mutual breach since 1974) is now the load-bearing claim — and that finding is what the federal government is appealing, so it has institutional weight.
final_self_check.underclaim_check: We do NOT soften the 48-year non-review or the High Court's 'breach of constitutional duty' finding — both are now in the cards explicitly.
final_self_check.misleading_framing_check: The hook now sets up BOTH pauses AND the 48-year backdrop in one card. The reframe attributes mutual breach to the court (not editorialised), and the view names the structural asymmetry without naming villains. The Sabah-side backings card is still on the page so the reader sees the political coalition behind the AKPS deferral.
final_self_check.unverified_detail_check: Removed verbatim 'fait accompli'. Removed verbatim 'untouched'. Removed 'joint legal review'. Removed 'Article IX' from sources. Added petroleum-exclusion claim with primary-source verification. View's 'unconstitutional' is the High Court's own characterisation, verified.
final_self_check.3R_special_caution: Unchanged from Stage 1: none of Race, Religion, or Royalty are engaged. Both threads are jurisdictional/constitutional between federal and Sabah state governments.
final_self_check.process_not_people_check: Strengthened. The High Court is now the load-bearing voice in the reframe ('the court found'). No politician is characterised. The structural-asymmetry framing critiques mechanisms, not actors.
final_self_check.anger_anxiety_check: Maintained: anxiety-of-precedent dominant. The 48-year framing intensifies the precedent risk without converting to anger-at-people.
